description: Standard: three summary formats for a feature

input.article_text: The city's public transit system will go fare-free starting July 1, making it the largest US municipality to eliminate fares entirely. The decision, approved unanimously by the transit board last week, will cost an estimated $42 million annually in lost fare revenue — offset by a new quarter-cent sales tax and $18 million in federal climate infrastructure grants. Proponents argue that fare-free transit reduces car dependency, improves air quality, and removes a barrier for low-income residents who spend a disproportionate share of their income on transportation. Three smaller cities that adopted fare-free models between 2020 and 2023 reported ridership increases of 20 to 35 percent, though critics note that none of those cities had systems as large or as financially complex as this one. The transit workers' union supports the move but has demanded additional funding for maintenance and staffing.
input.headline: No Fare: City Transit Goes Free in July

input.article_text: The library board voted Tuesday to extend weekend hours at all 12 branches starting in April, funded by a $1.2 million state grant.

description: European: standfirst for EU policy article

input.article_text: The European Commission has proposed mandatory climate risk disclosure for all companies with more than 250 employees operating in the EU single market, extending the existing Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive to cover an additional 50,000 firms. Under the proposal, companies must publish annual assessments of how climate change affects their operations, supply chains, and financial projections. The measure, if approved by the Parliament and Council, would take effect in 2028. Business groups warn of compliance costs exceeding €8 billion across the bloc.
input.headline: Europe's Climate Disclosure Rules Are About to Get Much Bigger
